longDescription:
Lower Galilee, Israel Mount Tabor (Hebrew: Har Tavor) is a distinctive, dome-shaped mountain rising 575 meters (1,886 feet)
                above the Jezreel Valley in Lower Galilee. Revered as the traditional site of Jesus Christ's
                Transfiguration - where he appeared in divine glory before Peter, James, and John, conversing
                with Moses and Elijah - Mount Tabor has been a place of Christian pilgrimage since at least
                the 4th century CE. Its isolated position, rising dramatically from the surrounding plain,
                has made it a natural sacred site since ancient times, mentioned in the Hebrew Bible in
                connection with the prophetess Deborah and the boundary of tribal territories.

description:
Explore temples, mountains, groves, and holy sites that have served as gateways between the human and divine realms
                across cultures and millennia. From ancient stone circles to living pilgrimage destinations, discover the places
                where heaven touches earth.

geography: Elevated terrain with commanding views; often snow-capped or shrouded in mist, symbolizing proximity to the divine

keyMyths.title: The Transfiguration of Jesus
keyMyths.description: According to the Synoptic Gospels, Jesus took Peter, James, and John to a high mountain (traditionally identified as Mount Tabor) where he was transfigured, his face shining like the sun and his clothes becoming dazzling white. Moses and Elijah appeared, speaking with Jesus, and a voice from heaven declared, "This is my beloved Son, with whom I am well pleased; listen to him."

keyMyths.title: Debora and Barak's Victory
keyMyths.description: In the Book of Judges, the prophetess Deborah summoned Barak to lead the Israelite army against Sisera, the commander of Jabin's Canaanite army. Deborah instructed Barak to gather ten thousand men from the tribes of Naphtali and Zebulun and meet her at Mount Tabor, where God would deliver Sisera into his hand. This battle is seen as a key victory for the Israelites.

keyMyths.title: Possible Site of the Battle of Mount Tabor (Napoleon)
keyMyths.description: While not a religious myth, the plain surrounding Mount Tabor was the site of a significant battle between Napoleon's forces and the Ottoman army in 1799. Some link this battle to biblical prophecy and the eschatological significance of the region, giving it a pseudo-mythological status in some interpretations.

keyMyths.title: Possible site of the resurrection appearance of Jesus
keyMyths.description: Although contested by many scholars, some traditions hold that Mount Tabor might have been the mountain where Jesus told his disciples to meet him after his resurrection. This is based on the prominence of the mountain and the lack of specific location given in the Gospels.

keyMyths.title: Association with the Messiah's Return
keyMyths.description: In some Jewish and Christian eschatological traditions, Mount Tabor is associated with the future return of the Messiah and the establishment of God's kingdom on earth. This is often linked to the mountain's earlier significance as a site of divine manifestation and military victory.
keyMyths.source: Various Jewish and Christian commentaries on messianic prophecy

sources.name: The Holy Bible
sources.description: Primary source for the accounts of the Transfiguration, Deborah and Barak, and possible resurrection appearance. Includes the Old and New Testaments.
sources.title: The Holy Bible

sources.name: Josephus, Flavius. Antiquities of the Jews.
sources.description: Provides historical context for the region and mentions Mount Tabor in relation to various events.
sources.title: Josephus, Flavius. Antiquities of the Jews.

sources.name: The Catholic Encyclopedia
sources.description: A comprehensive resource on Catholic teachings and history, including information on Mount Tabor's significance.
sources.title: The Catholic Encyclopedia

sources.name: New Schaff-Herzog Encyclopedia of Religious Knowledge
sources.description: A multi-volume encyclopedia covering a wide range of religious topics, including biblical geography and history.
sources.title: New Schaff-Herzog Encyclopedia of Religious Knowledge

sources.name: Murphy-O'Connor, Jerome. The Holy Land: An Oxford Archaeological Guide from Earliest Times to 1700.
sources.description: Provides archaeological and historical information about Mount Tabor and its surrounding area.
sources.title: Murphy-O'Connor, Jerome. The Holy Land: An Oxford Archaeological Guide from Earliest Times to 1700.
